events.title: The Rovers of Salé at Their Height
events.note: From the bar of the Bou Regreg the corsair republic of Salé sends its shallow-draught xebecs against Christian shipping from the Channel to the Newfoundland banks. Captives are sold in the souks; the rovers answer to no sultan but their own divan.

events.title: The Vergulde Draeck Strikes the Southland
events.note: Running the Brouwer route east before the westerlies, the VOC jacht Vergulde Draeck strikes a reef off the unknown Southland on 28 April 1656, with eight chests of coin aboard. Seventy-five souls reach the beach; a boat crew sails for Batavia, but the rescue ships never find the rest.

events.title: Myngs Storms Santiago de Cuba
events.note: In October 1662 Commodore Christopher Myngs leads the Port Royal squadron and a swarm of buccaneers against Santiago de Cuba, landing his men and taking the town from the rear. The Morro castle is blown up with its own powder, and the plunder confirms Jamaica's new trade of war.

events.title: L'Olonnais Ravages Maracaibo
events.note: François l'Olonnais brings a Tortuga fleet of eight sail and above six hundred men into the Lake of Maracaibo, putting Maracaibo and Gibraltar to weeks of plunder and torture. Exquemelin, who sailed with the buccaneers, records cruelties that made the Frenchman's name a terror on the Main.

events.title: Morgan Takes Portobelo
events.note: In July 1668 Henry Morgan lands some four hundred and sixty men from canoes by night and storms the castles of Portobelo, the treasure port of the Isthmus. The President of Panama is made to ransom his own town for a hundred thousand pesos of eight.

events.title: Morgan's Fireship at the Maracaibo Bar
events.note: Trapped inside the Lake of Maracaibo by the Armada de Barlovento in the spring of 1669, Morgan sends a disguised fireship into the Spanish flagship Magdalen and burns her at her anchors. A feigned landing draws the fort's guns landward, and the buccaneers slip the bar by night with their plunder.

events.title: Panama Burns
events.note: On 28 January 1671, after a starving march up the Chagres and across the Isthmus, Morgan's army breaks the Spanish militia on the savanna before Panama. The richest city of the South Sea burns to its stones — fired, the Spaniards said, by their own retreating garrison — and is never rebuilt on that ground.

events.title: The French Fleet Lost on Las Aves
events.note: On the night of 11 May 1678 the Comte d'Estrées, steering to surprise Dutch Curaçao, runs his battle fleet onto the reefs of Las Aves; seven ships of the line and a crowd of transports are lost. The buccaneers sailing with him salvage wine and stores from the wrecks, and Grammont leads the survivors off to raid Maracaibo instead.
sources: Crouse, The French Struggle for the West Indies

events.title: Buccaneer Canoes Win the Battle of Perico
events.note: On St George's Day, 23 April 1680, buccaneers who had crossed the Isthmus on foot attack the Spanish armadilla off Panama in canoes and piraguas, taking the barks after bloody boarding work. The victory gives Bartholomew Sharp and his fellows a squadron in the South Sea and opens a year of raiding along the Pacific coast.
sources: Ringrose, in Exquemelin, The Buccaneers of America

events.title: De Graaf and Grammont Sack Veracruz
events.note: On 17–18 May 1683 Laurens de Graaf, the Chevalier de Grammont and Yankey Willems land near a thousand filibusters by night and seize Veracruz, the treasure port of New Spain, herding its people into the churches. The town is held to ransom for days while the plate fleet rides helpless in the offing.

events.title: Spanish Razzia on New Providence
events.note: In January 1684 a Spanish raiding force out of Havana under Juan de Alarcón descends on Charles Town on New Providence, burning the settlement and carrying off prisoners in reprisal for English wrecking and piracy. A second razzia in 1686 finishes the work, leaving the island nearly empty until its rebirth as Nassau.
sources: Craton, A History of the Bahamas

events.title: The Golden Fleece Cornered in Samaná Bay
events.note: In June 1686 the frigates Falcon and Drake catch the pirate Joseph Bannister careening his Golden Fleece in Samaná Bay and batter her for two days, losing above twenty men to his shore guns. Bannister escapes into the woods, but his great ship is burnt; within the year he hangs from a Royal Navy yardarm in sight of Port Royal.
sources: Hanna, Pirate Nests and the Rise of the British Empire

events.title: The Port Royal Earthquake
events.note: Towards noon on 7 June 1692 the earth opens beneath Port Royal and two-thirds of the wickedest city in the English Americas slides into the harbour, its sand turned to quicksand. Some two thousand die in the minutes of the shock and thousands more of injury and pestilence after; preachers across two oceans call it judgment.
sources: Contemporary letters in Pawson & Buisseret, Port Royal, Jamaica

events.title: Thomas Tew Killed at the Red Sea Mouth
events.note: In September 1695, cruising the Bab-el-Mandeb in company with Henry Every's squadron, Thomas Tew of Newport attacks the Mughal ship Fateh Muhammed and is killed by a cannon shot that tears open his belly. The Amity's men strike, and the pioneer of the Pirate Round dies where he had made his fortune.
sources: Johnson, A General History of the Pyrates (1724)

events.title: Every Takes the Ganj-i-Sawai
events.note: In early September 1695 Henry Every's Fancy runs down the great Mughal pilgrim ship Ganj-i-Sawai off the Indian coast; a lucky shot brings down her mainmast and a powder burst clears her decks, and after a savage fight she yields. The plunder is reckoned the richest of the age, the outrages aboard bring Aurangzeb's wrath upon the East India Company, and Every vanishes from history.
sources: East India Company correspondence, in Earle, The Pirate Wars
Johnson, A General History of the Pyrates (1724)

events.title: Kidd Takes the Quedagh Merchant
events.note: On 30 January 1698, off the Malabar coast, William Kidd's leaking Adventure Galley takes the Armenian-freighted Quedagh Merchant under colour of French passes. The prize makes his fortune and his ruin: word of it travels home faster than he does, and the privateer of the Whig lords becomes the most wanted pirate alive.

events.title: Kidd Hanged at Execution Dock
events.note: On 23 May 1701 William Kidd is hanged at Execution Dock, Wapping, within the flood mark of the Thames; the first rope breaks and he is turned off a second time. His tarred body swings in chains at Tilbury Point for years after, a sermon to every outward-bound sailor.

events.title: Bowen Loses the Speaker on Mauritius
events.note: In January 1702 John Bowen's pirate ship Speaker, a captured slaver mounting fifty guns, drives onto the reefs of eastern Mauritius by night. The Dutch governor treats with the castaways rather than fight them, and Bowen buys a sloop and returns to the Madagascar trade.
sources: Johnson, A General History of the Pyrates (1724)
Excavation reports of the Speaker wreck (Lizé)

events.title: The Great Storm Sweeps the Goodwins
events.note: On the night of 26–27 November 1703 the greatest storm in English memory bursts over the Channel and drives the fleet in the Downs onto the Goodwin Sands; the Stirling Castle, Restoration, Northumberland and Mary are swallowed with above a thousand seamen. Daylight shows men clinging to the drying sands, waiting for boats or the returning tide.
sources: Rodger, The Command of the Ocean

events.title: Shovell's Fleet Wrecked on the Scillies
events.note: On the foul night of 22 October 1707, homeward from Toulon and out in its reckoning of longitude, Sir Cloudesley Shovell's fleet drives onto the rocks of Scilly; the Association, Eagle and Romney are lost with near two thousand men, the admiral among them. The disaster will goad Parliament toward the Longitude Act and its great prize.
sources: Rodger, The Command of the Ocean

events.title: Wager's Action: the San José Blows Up
events.note: On 8 June 1708 Commodore Charles Wager falls upon the Tierra Firme treasure fleet off Barú, near Cartagena. The galleon San José, deep-laden with the silver of Peru, takes fire and blows up in the dusk with near six hundred men, carrying one of the richest cargoes ever floated to the sea floor.
sources: Rodger, The Command of the Ocean

events.title: The Zuytdorp Vanishes on the Brouwer Route
events.note: Sailing the great-circle run east from the Cape in 1712, the VOC ship Zuytdorp never makes Batavia; no boat, letter or survivor ever arrives. Her coin and broken hull lie at the foot of the wild cliffs of the Southland, where later ages find relics suggesting some of her people lived ashore among them.
sources: Playford, Carpet of Silver: The Wreck of the Zuytdorp

events.title: The 1715 Plate Fleet Hurricane
events.note: Before dawn on 31 July 1715 a hurricane catches the combined treasure fleets of Ubilla and Echeverz in the Florida Strait and drives eleven ships onto the lee shore between the Sebastian and St Lucie inlets; perhaps a thousand men drown amid the breaking silver. Word of plate strewn in the shallows draws every rogue sloop in the Americas, and the great pirate decade begins on this beach.

events.title: Jennings Raids the Spanish Salvage Camps
events.note: In January 1716 Henry Jennings of Jamaica lands some three hundred men at the Palmar de Ays camp, where Spanish divers have been fishing silver from the 1715 wrecks, and carries off about eighty-seven thousand pieces of eight without a battle. Disowned by Jamaica for the outrage, Jennings and his consorts make for New Providence — among them, by repute, a young Charles Vane.

events.title: The Whydah Lost in the Cape Cod Storm
events.note: On the night of 26 April 1717 a violent nor'easter drives Samuel Bellamy's prize galley Whydah, heavy with the plunder of fifty ships, onto the bars off the back shore of Cape Cod. Bellamy and above a hundred and forty men drown within musket shot of the beach; two from the Whydah live, and six of the survivors are tried at Boston.
sources: The Trials of Eight Persons Indited for Piracy (Boston, 1717)

events.title: Blackbeard Blockades Charleston
events.note: For near a week in late May 1718 Edward Thatch lies off the Charleston bar with the Queen Anne's Revenge and his flotilla, stopping eight or nine ships and holding leading citizens hostage. His ransom for the town is not gold but a chest of medicines, delivered while the colony's shipping dares not stir.
sources: Johnson, A General History of the Pyrates (1724)

events.title: Woodes Rogers Arrives at Nassau
events.note: On 26–27 July 1718 Woodes Rogers, the King's new Governor, stands into Nassau harbour with his little squadron and the royal pardon. Charles Vane answers by firing a captured prize as a fireship at the warships and escaping out the eastern channel under black colours, while Hornigold and hundreds more come ashore to take the Act of Grace — the pirate republic's end begins.
sources: Johnson, A General History of the Pyrates (1724)

events.title: Battle of Cape Fear: Bonnet Taken
events.note: On 27 September 1718 Colonel William Rhett's sloops Henry and Sea Nymph ground alongside Stede Bonnet's Royal James in the shoals of the Cape Fear River, and the crews fire into one another for five hours until the tide refloats Rhett first. The gentleman pirate surrenders, is carried to Charleston, and after trial before Judge Trott hangs at White Point that December.
sources: The Tryals of Major Stede Bonnet and Other Pirates (London, 1719)
Johnson, A General History of the Pyrates (1724)

events.title: Battle of Ocracoke: Blackbeard Falls
events.note: At dawn on 22 November 1718 Lieutenant Robert Maynard's hired sloops work into Ocracoke Inlet and board Edward Thatch's Adventure after a broadside that near wrecks them. Blackbeard dies on Maynard's deck with five pistol wounds and a score of cuts, and his head is carried home swinging from the bowsprit.
sources: Johnson, A General History of the Pyrates (1724)

events.title: Hornigold the Pirate-Hunter and the Nassau Hangings
events.note: Benjamin Hornigold, lately the senior pirate of the Bahamas, takes the pardon and Rogers' commission, and in late 1718 hunts down John Auger's gang of relapsed pirates among the out-islands. On 12 December 1718 the convicted men hang on the Nassau waterfront before their old shipmates — proof that the Act of Grace cuts both ways.

events.title: Rackham, Bonny and Read Taken off Negril
events.note: In late October 1720 Jonathan Barnet's privateer sloop runs down Calico Jack Rackham's William at anchor off Negril Point by night. Witnesses swear the men gave little fight, and that the fiercest resistance came from Anne Bonny and Mary Read, cursing on deck while their shipmates kept below.
sources: The Tryals of Captain John Rackam and Other Pirates (Jamaica, 1721)

events.title: The Jamaica Trials: Rackham Hanged, the Women Plead Their Bellies
events.note: At St Jago de la Vega in November 1720 the Admiralty court condemns Rackham and his crew; he hangs at Gallows Point on the 18th, his tarred body set on the cay off Port Royal that bears his name. Tried days later, Anne Bonny and Mary Read are likewise condemned but plead their bellies, and the court stays the rope for the children they carry.
sources: The Tryals of Captain John Rackam and Other Pirates (Jamaica, 1721)

events.title: Charles Vane Hanged at Gallows Point
events.note: Wrecked by a hurricane in the Bay of Honduras and betrayed by a chance acquaintance, Charles Vane — the last unpardoned captain of the Nassau republic — is carried to Jamaica, tried, and hanged at Gallows Point on 29 March 1721. His body is hung in chains at Gun Cay as a mark for shipping.
sources: Johnson, A General History of the Pyrates (1724)

events.title: Taylor and La Buse Take the Cabo
events.note: In April 1721 John Taylor's Cassandra and Olivier Levasseur's Victory stand into the road of Bourbon island and take the Portuguese carrack Nossa Senhora do Cabo, lying dismasted after a storm with the retiring Viceroy of Goa aboard. Her diamonds and church plate make it perhaps the richest single prize of the entire pirate age.
sources: Johnson, A General History of the Pyrates (1724)
Grey, Pirates of the Eastern Seas

events.title: Battle of Cape Lopez: Black Bart Falls
events.note: On 10 February 1722 HMS Swallow, Captain Chaloner Ogle, comes up with Bartholomew Roberts' Royal Fortune at Cape Lopez on the Guinea coast. Roberts, dressed in crimson damask with a diamond cross at his neck, is killed by grapeshot in the first broadsides, and his crew throw his body overboard in his arms and armour as he had asked.
sources: Johnson, A General History of the Pyrates (1724)

events.title: The Cape Coast Castle Trials
events.note: In March and April 1722 the survivors of Roberts' company — above two hundred and sixty men — are tried by Admiralty court within the walls of Cape Coast Castle; fifty-two are hanged, many in chains along the shore, in the greatest mass execution of the war on piracy. Others are sold to the mines or carried home, and the terror of the verdict echoes through every forecastle in the Atlantic.
sources: A Full and Exact Account of the Tryal of All the Pyrates Lately Taken by Captain Ogle (1723)

events.title: Edward Low's Rampage Checked by the Greyhound
events.note: On 10 June 1723, in the height of a cruise marked by tortures that sickened even pirate witnesses, Edward Low's two sloops engage HMS Greyhound off Block Island. Low abandons his consort Ranger and flies; Charles Harris and his men are taken, and twenty-six of them hang together at Newport that July.
sources: The Tryals of Thirty-Six Persons for Piracy (Newport, 1723)
Johnson, A General History of the Pyrates (1724)

events.title: La Buse Hanged on Bourbon
events.note: On 7 July 1730 Olivier Levasseur, called La Buse, last great captain of the Indian Ocean pirates, is hanged at Saint-Paul on Bourbon island for the taking of the Cabo nine years before. Legend — though not the record — has him flinging a cipher to the crowd with the cry that his treasure should go to him who could read it.
sources: Grey, Pirates of the Eastern Seas
